segments.s413-g41: نشست is a third-person singular past tense meaning “he sat/settled.” The following prepositional sequence از بر باره is an archaic/poetic layering of prepositions (از ... بر ...) marking position or separation “off/from the side of,” with باره functioning as a local/occasional noun; بادپای is the noun being located (‘the swift-footed one’). Word order is verb → prepositional phrase (verb first, then the location).
segments.s413-g42: پراندیشه is a substantive/epithet “the pensive/ thoughtful (one).” از کوه is a simple prepositional phrase “from the mountain.” شد is the past auxiliary/verb marking the action (here functioning like “was/became” → idiomatically “moved/turned”); باز modifies the verb as “again/back,” and جای is the noun “place.” The sequence literally reads “became back place,” i.e. “returned to his place.”
segments.s413-g43: بگفت is a past-tense verb 'he said/told' (the prefix بـ is common in older Persian past forms). آن شگفتی is the direct object ‘that marvel/wonder,’ and به موبد is the indirect object ‘to the mobed.’ The relative clause که دید (‘which he saw’) follows to qualify the marvel; the clause order is object → indirect object → relative clause.
segments.s413-g44: وزان is a connective particle meaning ‘and/from that/thereupon’ linking to the previous remark. راه آسان is a noun phrase ‘the easy path.’ سر اندر کشید is an idiomatic sequence: سر (head) + اندر (into/within) + کشید (past of کشیدن ‘to draw/pull’), literally ‘drew (his) head into,’ here meaning ‘withdrew/entered (himself) into’ or ‘moved into (the path).’ Note: the supplied sense list for کشید does not include the usual ‘to draw/pull,’ so its sense index is left unspecified below.
segments.s413-g45: چو آمد is a temporal clause ‘when he came/arrived’ (چو = when). به نزدیک + نخچیرگاه is a locative phrase ‘near the hunting-ground.’ This is a standard past-tense temporal clause that sets the scene for the main action.
segments.s413-g46: هم‌انگه is an adverb ‘at that very moment.’ تهمتن is the subject name (Tahmētan). بدیدش is the past verb بدید ‘saw’ with the enclitic object ‑ش ‘him’ (verb + object clitic). ز راه is a simple prepositional phrase ‘from/on the road.’ Word order is adverb → subject → verb+object → locative.
segments.s413-g47: به موبد چنین گفت places the indirect object before the verb: ‘he said thus to the mobed.’ The following clause کین مرد کیست is a direct question/quotation: کین is an archaic demonstrative ‘this’ (literally ‘that/this one’), مرد ‘man,’ and کیست is the copular interrogative ‘is who?’ (literally ‘who is (he)?’). Note: the lemma senses provided for گفت and کین in the prompt do not include the straightforward verbal/demonstrative senses used here, so those are marked below as unspecified.
segments.s413-g48: من is the speaker ‘I.’ ایدون is an archaic/poetic particle here introducing the speaker’s immediate reaction (something like ‘now/seeing him’ or ‘on seeing him’); its exact sense is archaic and not covered by the supplied senses. گمانم is present-tense ‘I suppose/I think’ (گمان + enclitic ‑م). که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that he is Goshtāsp,’ and گشتاسپیست is the predicate ‘is Goshtāsp’ (گشتاسپی + copula ‑ست).
segments.s413-g65: سراپرده (the inner curtain/entrance curtain) is the direct object/foreground noun and زد is the past tense 3rd-person singular of زدن used here in the sense 'to strike/draw aside' (a common poetic idiom: پرده زدن = to draw or move a curtain). بر لب هیرمند is a prepositional phrase 'on the bank of the Helmand' (لب = 'brink/bank') that locates the action; word order is verb after its object phrase because of the poetic, somewhat inverted syntax.
segments.s413-g66: به فرمان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by the command of'; فرخنده شاه بلند is a sequence of epithets qualifying the king (فرخنده 'auspicious', شاه 'king', بلند 'lofty/high'), with the adjectives and honorifics in front of or around شاه in typical poetic order.
segments.s413-g67: پیامی is the accusative object 'a message' and رسانم is 1st-person singular present/intentional form 'I deliver/I will deliver' (classical Persian uses present to express near-future or intention). ز اسفندیار is the source prepositional phrase 'from Esfandiyar' (ز = 'from').
segments.s413-g68: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if'; بشنود is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'he hears / should hear' (typical in formal/classical conditional constructions); پهلوان سوار is a noun phrase 'the mounted champion/hero' (پهلوان = champion, سوار = rider/mounted), with the two nouns forming a compound epithet.
segments.s413-g69: چنین گفت رستم is the formula 'thus said Rostam' (چنین 'thus/so', گفت is the past verb 'said' used here; رستم is the subject). The following که begins the content clause that gives the substance of what he said (که = 'that'). فرمان شاه is a genitive/construct 'the king's command'.
segments.s413-g70: برآنم is a fixed verbal phrase 'I am resolved / I am intent upon' (بر + آن + م personal ending), followed by که introducing the subordinate clause. برتر ز خورشید و ماه is a comparative phrase 'higher/superior than the sun and the moon' where ز is the poetic comparative particle 'than' and the adjective برتر precedes it as is common in Persian poetic word-order.
segments.s413-g71: خوریم is 1st-person plural present/potential 'we eat / we will eat' (used here to mean 'we will eat'); آنچ داریم is a relative/nominal phrase 'that which we have' (آنچ = 'that which'; داریم = 'we have'); چیزی نخست places an adverbial emphasis 'something, first' — overall meaning 'we'll eat first what we have.'
segments.s413-g72: پس‌انگه is an adverb 'afterwards/then'; جهان زیر فرمان تست is a nominal clause 'the world is under (your) command' where زیر فرمان = 'under the command (of)' and تست is the 2nd-person singular copula/pronoun-form meaning 'is yours / is under you' (an archaic poetic ending).
segments.s413-g97: یکی here = 'someone' (indefinite subject) preceding the object. جام زرین is a noun phrase 'a golden cup' (noun + adjective). پر از باده کرد uses پر + از + noun to mean 'filled with X' and کرد is the simple past of کردن 'to do/make' used causatively = 'filled (it) with'. Word order is Subject – Object – Predicate.
segments.s413-g98: وزو is an archaic/poetic combining form (likely و + ز او = 'and from it') linking this clause to the previous cup; یاد ... کرد is a light-verb construction 'to cause remembrance / to recall' (یاد + کردن = 'to remind/evoke the memory of'). مردان آزاده is the object 'the free/noble men' being recalled.
segments.s413-g99: دگر = 'another' (an alternative/next item). جام is the object and بر دست بهمن is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto Bahman's hand' (بر + دست + proper name). نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put' = 'he placed'.
segments.s413-g100: که introduces the reported imperative/exhortation. برگیر is an imperative/infinitive meaning 'take up / take' directed to the addressee. ازان کس = 'from that person' (ازان = 'from that', کس = 'person'). که خواهی تو یاد is a relative clause modifying کس: 'whom you wish to remember' (خواهی is 2nd-person present 'you want', تو is the emphatic subject; یاد = 'memory/remembering').
segments.s413-g101: بترسید is the past of ترسیدن with the older prefixal form (here 'he feared / was afraid'). بهمن is the personal name (subject). ز + جام نبید = 'of the cup of wine' (poetic preposition ز = از). نبید is a word for wine; the phrase expresses Bahman's fear of that cup.
segments.s413-g102: نخستین دمی = 'at the first moment/instant' (adverbial phrase). درکشید is the past of درکشیدن meaning 'drew in / inhaled / took (a draught)'; the object (a sip/breath) is implicit. زواره here is uncertain/ambiguous in this line (likely an archaic connective or name) and does not fit the provided sense list directly.
segments.s413-g103: بدو گفت is the common order 'to him he said' (indirect object + past verb). کای is the vocative particle 'O' used to address someone. بچه شهریار is the vocative phrase 'child/offspring of the king' (here 'son/prince').
segments.s413-g104: به تو شاد بادا is an optative/final construction 'may you be happy' (بادا = 'may it be so'). می‌و میگسار are two nouns placed together as the wished-for items — archaic/poetic words for 'wine' (می‌و) and 'cupbearer / wine-companion' (میگسار) — so the speaker wishes the addressee happiness through wine and a cupbearer.
segments.s413-g33: This is a nominal+verbal clause in which غمی (a noun 'a grief') is the logical subject and شد (past of شدن, 'became') is the verb meaning 'came to be / seized'. دل بهمن is the possessor phrase 'Bahman's heart' (دل = heart) and از کار اوی gives the cause: از here = 'because of' + کار اوی = 'his deed'. Word order (Noun + شد + possessor + cause) is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s413-g34: چو دید is a temporal clause 'when he saw' (چو = 'when', دید = past 'saw'). آن بزرگی و کردار اوی is the object of that seeing: آن modifies بزرگی = 'that greatness' and کردار اوی = 'his conduct/deeds' (اوی = 'his'). The conjunction و simply links بزرگی and کردار.
segments.s413-g35: همی گفت‌گر is a classical formula meaning roughly 'one might say' or 'it may be said' (همی an emphatic/old particle; گفت‌گر functions as a reporting/hypothetical phrase). فرخ اسفندیار is the noun phrase 'fortunate Esfandiyar' (فرخ as epithet + proper name). The whole segment introduces a reported/hypothetical statement.
segments.s413-g36: کند is the present/subjunctive/optative use of کردن here meaning 'would do / could do / would engage in'. با چنین نامور کارزار is the object phrase: با = 'with' but idiomatically 'in/engage in' + چنین نامور = 'such famed' + کارزار = 'battle/struggle'. The verb at the end governs the whole object phrase.
segments.s413-g37: This clause has the object تن خویش 'his own body' (here written as تن + خوی + ش, where خویش = 'one's own') followed by در جنگ 'in battle' showing location/context, رسوا 'disgraced/exposed' as predicative adjective, and کند (present/polite/habitual) as the verb 'does/would bring about'. The word order places the object and circumstance before the final verb, as common in Persian verse.
segments.s413-g38: همان به که is an idiomatic evaluative phrase meaning 'it is better that / it is preferable that'. با او مدارا کند is a subordinate clause with با او = 'with him' and مدارا کند = 'deal gently / show tolerance' (کند again as present/subjunctive). Overall: 'it is better that he treat him gently.'
segments.s413-g39: ور is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a classical/poetic particle meaning roughly 'perhaps/if by chance' (here strengthening the conditional). او بهتر آید به جنگ is a small clause where او is the person concerned, بهتر آید literally 'it comes better' i.e. 'it seems better', and به جنگ 'to fight' marks the option being evaluated — so 'if it seems better to him to fight...'.
segments.s413-g40: همه شهر ایران is the direct object 'all the cities of Iran' (همه modifies شهر). بگیرد is the present/future-like subjunctive/indicative of گرفتن 'will seize / takes', and به چنگ is an idiom meaning 'into his grip' or 'into his power' (چنگ = grasp/claw). Word order places the object before the verb as typical in poetry.
segments.s413-g81: This clause lists three coordinated actions (implicit subject “they”): نمک بر پراگند = “(they) scattered salt on (it)” (نمک + بر = salt placed on), ببرید = a past/action verb (‘they took/seized/cut off’ in context ‘cut up’) and خورد = ‘(they) ate’. The order is verb phrases joined by و; هم‌زمینهٔ اپیتتیک و فاقد ضمیر صریح است (typical of epic Persian).
segments.s413-g82: نظاره بروبر سرافراز مرد is a nominal clause: نظاره بروبر = ‘a straight/a face‑to‑face sight/looking’ (adverbial phrase) modifying the following noun; سرافراز مرد = ‘the triumphant/lofty man’. The order is poetic (oblique adverbial + epithet + noun): the man watches (is the one doing the looking) and his attitude is سرافراز.
segments.s413-g83: همی خورد indicates a continuous/habitual past action (همی = still/kept on), with بهمن as the subject: ‘Bahman kept eating’. ز گور = ‘from the grave’ (ز = from, poetic preposition) and اندکی = ‘a little’ (adverbial qualifier).
segments.s413-g84: This clause states a negative existential fact. نبد = negated ‘was not / did not happen’ (archaic/poetic), خوردنش = ‘his/its eating’ (the nominalized verb = ‘the act of eating it’), followed by a prepositional phrase زان او صد یکی = ‘of that, one of his hundred’. Together: ‘There was not one of his hundred who ate it.’
segments.s413-g85: بخندید is a simple past third‑person ‘he/they laughed’; رستم is the subject named; بدو گفت = ‘said to him’ (بدو = to him, گفت is reported speech verb). The sequence = ‘Rostam laughed and said to him...’; شاه here is a vocative or addressee (‘O king’).
segments.s413-g86: ز بهر = a fixed archaic prepositional phrase ‘for the sake of / because of’; خورش is the object ‘stew/dish’; دارد is present/past‑narrative auxiliary ‘has/provides’; این پیشگاه is the locative/agentive phrase ‘this court/presence’. Word order is inverted poetically: literal ‘for the stew this court has’ → natural English: ‘this court provides that stew.’
segments.s413-g87: خورش = ‘the stew/dish’; چون = ‘since/as/if’ introducing a concessive/causal clause; بدین گونه (here split in the lexicon list) = ‘in this way/such’ (بدین = ‘in this manner’, گونه = ‘kind/manner’); داری = second‑person present/past‑narrative ‘you have’; به خوان = ‘at the table/feast’. The clause questions the purpose of going when such a dish is already at hand.
segments.s413-g88: چرا = interrogative ‘why’; رفتی = second‑person past ‘you went’; اندر دم = archaic locative/temporal phrase ‘in the moment/while in the midst of’; هفتخوان = a fixed mythic compound ‘the seven‑chambers’ (i.e. seven trials). Together: ‘Why did you go into the Seven Trials?’
segments.s413-g17: بترسم is 1st person singular expressing fear (present/subjunctive nuance) and introduces a subordinate clause with که 'that'. The string با او places the encounteral/against sense of با before the noun phrase یل اسفندیار, so با او یل اسفندیار = 'in facing him, the hero Esfandiyar'. Word order is topic (with/against him) + appositive title.
segments.s413-g18: نتابد is the 3rd-person negative present (or narrative form) of تابیدن here with the sense 'to endure/withstand' and depends on the earlier که. بپیچد is a 3rd-person verb ('to twist/turn') used with سر as its object; the verb comes before the noun for dramatic effect. از marks separation/'from' and کارزار names the place/event ('battle'). Together: 'will not endure and his head will be twisted off from the battle.'
segments.s413-g19: من is the emphatic subject 'I'. این را is the definite object ('this' + object marker را) referring back to the foe; به یک سنگ here means 'with/by a single stone' (به as instrumental). بیجان is a predicate/adjective 'lifeless', and کنم is 1st-person singular of کردن used causatively/performatively: 'I will make/cause [him] lifeless.'
segments.s413-g20: دل زال و رودابه is the noun phrase 'the heart(s) of Zal and Rudabeh' (positional: object before verb). پیچان is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'twisting' or 'causing twisting/grief' and functions as the result/complement, and کنم repeats the 1st-person verb 'I will make/cause' to link this result to the same agent.
segments.s413-g21: یکی + سنگ is 'one/a single stone' (یکی emphasizing singularity). زان is the poetic form of 'from that' and introduces the source: کوه خارا 'the flinty/rocky mountain' (خارا as an adjectival descriptor). بکند is the 3rd-person narrative form of کندن here meaning 'he pulled/removed' (narrative past sense in the epic).
segments.s413-g22: فروهشت is a past/narrative verb meaning 'he cast/tore down, scattered' (used here to say he sent the stone down). زان again 'from that', and کوهسار بلند is a compound 'the high/towering mountainside' (کوهسار = mountain place, بلند = high). The verb precedes the rest for dramatic motion imagery.
segments.s413-g23: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. نخچیرگاهش is a single word 'his hunting-ground' (نخچیرگاه + possessive -ش). زواره is a proper name/place seen from that spot, and بدید is the past 3rd-person of دیدن 'he saw'. The order is 'from his hunting-ground he saw Zovareh'.
segments.s413-g24: خروشیدن is a deverbal noun/infinitive 'roaring' used as the object of شنید 'he heard'. سنگ خارا is 'the flinty stone' (سنگ + خارا, here خارا functions as noun/adjective 'flint/rock'), and شنید is the past 3rd-person 'he heard'. The clause means 'he heard the flinty stone roar.'
segments.s413-g25: خروشید is a past intransitive verb meaning 'a roar/cries rose' (verb before subject is common in older epic diction). کای is the archaic vocative particle 'O', and مهتر نامدار is the vocative noun phrase 'renowned chief' (مهتر 'chief' + نامدار 'renowned'). Together they report a shouted address: 'A cry rose: "O renowned chief!"'.
segments.s413-g26: یکی 'one/a single' modifies سنگ; غلتان شد is a compound where غلتان (rolling) + شد (became/turned) indicates the stone began to roll. از introduces the origin 'from' and کوهسار is the mountainside; word order places the participial description before شد and the source after از.
segments.s413-g27: نجنبید is a negative past-form verb 'did not move/stir' placed before the subject رستم (common inverted order in epic Persian). نه introduces the coordinated negative 'nor' for the following verb phrase; بنهاد (past of نهادن) takes the direct object گور ('grave') placed after it, giving 'nor did he lay/establish a grave.'
segments.s413-g28: زواره is the subject (proper name). همی is the old progressive particle meaning 'kept on/was still', and کرد is the past verb 'did/made' — together 'kept doing'. ازان گونه شور is the object phrase 'that sort of tumult/commotion' (ازان 'of that' + گونه 'manner' + شور 'commotion'), placed after the verb.
segments.s413-g29: همی بود is an imperfective/past progressive idiom meaning 'it was (continuing)'; تا ('until') introduces the subordinate clause. سنگ نزدیک شد is SOV-like here with the verb شد ('became') final: 'the stone became near' = 'the stone approached'.
segments.s413-g30: ز before گردش is a poetic preposition 'from/owing to', marking cause ('because of the turning/whirl'). بر کوه is a locative phrase 'upon the mountain', تاریک is the predicate adjective 'dark', and شد (final) is the past copula 'became' — 'the mountain became dark because of the whirl.'
segments.s413-g31: بزد is the simple past 'he struck'; پاشنه (heel) follows as the instrument (no preposition). سنگ is the direct object and بنداخت is another past verb 'he cast/threw' with دور as a post‑verbal adverb 'far'. The two finite verbs share the same (understood) subject.
segments.s413-g32: زواره is the subject. برو here can be read as a short movement/interjectional element (literary 'off/away' or 'go'), while آفرین کرد is a past verbal phrase 'he uttered praise/ cried “Bravo!”'. The final و پور is elliptical: و 'and' plus پور 'son' — an appositive or vocative left without an explicit verb (a common concise epic construction meaning roughly 'and (he said) “son!”').
segments.s413-g89: چگونه is an interrogative adverb 'how' placed at the start. زدی is the past tense (2nd/3rd person singular past of زدن) meaning 'you/he struck/thrust'; its direct object نیزه (spear) follows the verb. در کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'in battle' giving the setting.
segments.s413-g90: چو here functions as a subordinating conjunction 'when'. خوردن is used as a verbal noun/infinitive 'eating' (habit/consumption). چنین داری literally 'you have such' (= 'you are given to such eating'): داری is present 'you have/possess' (indicating a habitual quality). ای شهریار is the vocative 'O king' addressing the addressee.
segments.s413-g91: بدو is the clitic/old-fashioned object/indirect-object 'to him' placed before the verb. گفت here stands for the past 'said' (speech verb) with بهمن as the agent; که introduces the following content/appositive clause describing Khosrow.
segments.s413-g92: This clause is an optative/wish headed by a final مباد 'may it not be'. سخن‌گوی and بسیار خواره are predicative nouns/adjectival phrases joined by و 'and' describing the wished-about qualities; with مباد at the end the whole clause expresses a negative wish ('may he not be a talker and great eater').
segments.s413-g93: خورش (food/dish) is the subject followed by the compound predicate کم بود 'was little/insufficient' (past). کوشش و جنگ بیش lists two coordinated nouns 'effort and war' with بیش 'more/much' indicating abundance or emphasis—so the phrase contrasts meagre food with abundant exertion/warfare.
segments.s413-g94: به کف بر نهیم is a verbal phrase: به کف 'into the palm/hand', بر نهیم is the (classical) present/subjunctive 1st person plural of نهاد 'we put/let us put' (here rendered 'we will put' / 'let us put'). آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time' and جان خویش 'our/one's own life' is the object—together: 'then we will put our lives into our hands' (i.e., take the risk).
segments.s413-g95: بخندید is the past tense 'he laughed'; the subject رستم follows it (a common classical word order). به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice / aloud' modifying گفت, which is the speech verb 'said' introducing the following clause.
segments.s413-g96: که introduces the reported content. مردی is an indefinite noun 'a man'; نشاید is an impersonal/formal negative jussive 'it is not fitting/should not'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; مردان is the object 'men' (here 'other men' or 'one's fellows'); نهفت is a past/adjectival form 'be hidden/concealed'. The clause therefore means 'that a man ought not to be hidden from men.'
segments.s413-g57: Vara (ورا) is the object pronoun 'to him' placed before the subject; پهلوان is the subject 'the champion/hero'. زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the action. در بر گرفت is a fixed verb phrase (درِ بر گرفتن) meaning 'to take into one's arms / to embrace'; گرفت is simple past (3rd person) 'took'. Word order here is object – subject – adverb – verb phrase, which is acceptable in classical Persian for emphasis.
segments.s413-g58: ز introduces the reason/source here: 'because of / on account of'. دیر آمدن is a verbal noun (the act/occasion of 'coming late' — 'the late arrival'). پوزش is the noun 'apology/excuse' and اندر گرفت ('اندر گرفتن' + گرفت) functions as 'accepted/received' the excuse; گرفت is past tense. The whole phrase means 'he accepted the apology on account of the late arrival.'
segments.s413-g59: برفتند is past plural 'they went/left'. هر دو 'both' specifies the plural subject. به جای نشست is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'to their places / to their seats' (به + جای 'to the place' and نشست here understood as 'seat/sitting'); the verb 'sat' is implied in the idiom. The clause therefore means 'both went to their places / both returned to their seats.'
segments.s413-g60: خود here is a reflexive/emphatic 'himself' (or 'he himself') contrasted with the following group. و is the conjunction 'and'. نامداران is an adjective/noun meaning 'the renowned/illustrious ones' modifying خسروپرست, which is a compound epithet 'Khosrow‑worshippers' (men devoted to or sycophantic toward Khosrow). Together: 'he himself and the renowned Khosrow‑worshippers.'
segments.s413-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بنشست is past singular 'sat down' (here third person, 'he sat'). بهمن is the proper name and the subject who sat. بدادش درود is an object‑giving phrase: درود 'greeting/salutation' is the direct object; بدادش is an archaic prefixed past form meaning 'he gave to him' (here 'he gave him [the] greeting'). The lexicon entry provided for بدادش does not reflect this verbal use, so its listed senses do not match the usage in context.
segments.s413-g62: ز before شاه and again before ایرانیان marks source/'from' (i.e., 'from the king and from the Iranians'). برفزود is a past verb meaning 'he added / bestowed (increased)'; here it indicates that Bahman augmented the greeting by adding the salutations coming from the king and the Iranians.
segments.s413-g63: ازان پس is a two‑word temporal sequence 'after that / thereafter'. چنین گفت is 'thus said' or 'said (as) follows'; گفت is the past verb 'he said' (the provided lexicon senses list 'conversation' etc., which do not match the verb form here). کاسفندیار is the speaker's name. The whole segment sets up direct speech: 'After that Kasfandiyar thus said.'
segments.s413-g64: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آتش is 'fire' (here likely metaphorical). برفت is a past action 'went/rose/issued forth' but the lemma given in the input appears misindexed (the provided senses correspond to 'snow'); in context برفت functions as the past verb 'departed/rose'. از + در 'from the door/gate' (در here = 'door, gate') and شهریار = 'the king'. Together: 'when the fire went forth from the king's gate.'
segments.s413-g105: ازو is a prepositional phrase 'from him' placed before the verb. بستد is the simple past (3rd sg.) of بستن used here in the narrative sense 'seized/took' (poetic usage). آن جام is the direct object ('that cup'); بهمن follows as a proper name in apposition or possession ('the cup of Bahman'). به چنگ is a prepositional phrase ('into/into the grasp') with به meaning 'towards/into' and چنگ meaning 'claw/hand'—together: 'into his grasp.'
segments.s413-g106: دل‌آزار کرده is a participial phrase: دل‌آزار ('heart‑aching') + کرده (past participle of کردن) = 'having made (it) heart‑aching' or 'having caused sorrow.' بدان is the adverbial/locative 'به + دان' functioning as 'at/on that' (classical contraction), and می‌درنگ is the imperfective/presentive narrative verb (می‑ + درنگ) meaning 'he pauses / he lingers.' The whole clause says he lingered there, after/while making hearts ache.
segments.s413-g107: همی ماند combines the continuous narrative particle همی with the past stem ماند (ماندن) and yields a past narrative sense 'he remained / was left (continually).' از رستم here marks the thing/ person at which he is astonished — 'at/with respect to Rostam.' اندر شگفت is a classical prepositional phrase meaning 'in wonder/amazement' (اندر = 'in', شگفت = 'amazement').
segments.s413-g108: ازان is a deictic 'from that / because of that' linking back to the previous action. خوردن here functions as a verbal noun/infinitive used as 'the striking / the blow' (classical Persian often uses infinitives substantively). The repeated و lists the items that astonished him: یال (mane), بازوی (arm), کفت (palm of the hand). The word order is an enumerative list introduced by the deictic 'ازان.'
segments.s413-g109: نشستند is a 3rd person plural past narrative verb 'they sat / they took their seats / they mounted' (classical epic usage). بر باره is a prepositional phrase with بر 'on/upon' and باره (here) 'saddle/load/pack'—'upon the saddle/pack.' هر دو سوار follows as an identifying phrase 'each of the two riders' / 'both riders' specifying who sat.
segments.s413-g110: همی راند combines the narrative particle همی with the past stem راند (راندن) and means 'he rode/charged' (a habitual/narrative past sense). بهمن is the proper-name subject 'Bahman', and بر نامدار is a prepositional phrase 'against/upon the famed one' with بر marking the target and نامدار = 'renowned/famed.'
segments.s413-g111: بدادش is a past-tense verb complex 'داد + ش' meaning 'he gave him' (the dative clitic ش = 'to him'). یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' or 'each in turn.' درود و پیام are the coordinated direct objects 'greetings and messages.' The verb precedes its objects in the classical word order.
segments.s413-g112: از اسفندیار is a prepositional phrase 'from Esfandiyar' (از = 'from'). آن یل نیک‌نام is an appositive description 'that hero, well‑reputed' (آن = 'that', یل = 'hero', نیک‌نام = 'well‑named / renowned'), identifying Esfandiyar more fully.
segments.s413-g9: یکی introduces an indefinite/unspecified individual ('one' or 'someone'); جام پر می‌به is a noun phrase 'a cup full of wine' (پر = 'full (of)' modifies جام, می‌به = poetic 'wine'); دست دگر literally 'the hand of another' (دگر = 'another'), so the whole phrase means 'one (man) [with] a cup full of wine in another's/another man's hand' — more naturally 'one (man) held a cup full of wine in his hand.' Word order is typical older/poetic Persian where modifiers cluster and possession is expressed by juxtaposition rather than a modern possessive clitic.
segments.s413-g10: پرستنده is a noun 'an admirer/devotee' used appositionally to mark a different person; بر پای پیشش پسر is a locative phrase 'a youth at his feet before him' (بر = 'on/at', پای = 'foot', پیشش = 'before him/in his presence', پسر = 'youth/young man'). Persian often drops the copula, so the clause lists people/roles by juxtaposition: 'another, an admirer, [had] a youth at his feet.'
segments.s413-g11: همی is an iterative/progressive particle ('kept / was'), گشت is an intransitive past verb here meaning 'roamed/was going about', رخش is the subject 'his steed/warhorse', and اندران مرغزار is the locative 'in that meadow' (اندران = 'therein/in that'). Word order places the verb before its subject (poetic/older Persian), so: 'The steed was roaming in the meadow.'
segments.s413-g12: درخت و گیا (گیا = vegetation) is a coordinated nominal phrase 'trees and plants'; بود is the past copula/existential 'there was'; و هم جویبار adds 'and also a stream' with هم = 'also' and the verb understood from the previous clause. This is a simple existential listing: 'There were trees and plants, and also a stream.'
segments.s413-g13: به دل گفت is an idiom 'he said to his heart' meaning 'he thought to himself'; بهمن is the speaker's name. که introduces reported content ('that'); رستمست is a poetic contraction equivalent to رستم است ('is Rostam') where the copula is fused as -ست (archaic/poetic). Note: گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the provided senses list did not include the verb sense).
segments.s413-g14: وگر is used as 'or perhaps / or if' introducing an alternative conjecture; آفتاب سپیده is 'the sun of dawn' (sun + dawn); دمست is a poetic fusion (دم است) meaning 'it is the moment of dawn' or 'is (the) dawn's [light]' — here it functions as the predicate 'it is (the) sun at daybreak.' The line expresses Bahman's hesitation whether the figure is Rostam or merely the rising sun at dawn.
segments.s413-g15: به گیتی is a locative 'in the world'; کسی ... ندید with negation yields 'no one saw'; مرد is used as the noun 'a man' (object of ندید) and ازین سان means 'of/from this sort' or 'in this way' (ازین = 'from this', سان = 'manner/like'). So the clause says 'No one in the world saw a man like this.' Word order places locative first, then the indefinite subject, then object phrase and the negated verb.
segments.s413-g16: نه introduces the continuation 'nor'; از نامداران is 'of/from the renowned ones' (از = 'of/about' here), پیشی is an abstract noun 'precedence/superiority', and شنید is 'heard (of)' — together: 'nor did one hear that any of the renowned had precedence.' It means there was no report of any famous man being superior to him.
segments.s413-g73: بگسترد is an archaic/past third-person verb (he spread) formed with the prefixed بـ typical of epic narrative. The preposition بر (“on”) governs سفره (table-cloth / the laid spread), and the second بر links to نان نرم (on soft bread) — نان (bread) is modified by the adjective نرم (soft) following it, so the whole chunk means “he spread … on the table a soft bread.”
segments.s413-g74: این جمله مرکب دارای فاعل نامعین یکی (“someone / a certain one”) and the past verb بیاورد (he brought). گور here is a meat animal (onager) modified by بریان (roasted), and the final گرم (warm/hot) is an adjective/adverb describing how it was brought. Word order: subject (یکی) + object (گور بریان) + verb (بیاورد) + manner (گرم).
segments.s413-g75: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” دستارخوان is the agent (the chanter/ritual reader) and پیش بهمن is a prepositional phrase “before/in the presence of Bahman.” نهاد is a past verb (placed/put/took position) — here it functions as the main past-tense verb of the clause, so the clause reads “when the chanter placed himself/stood before Bahman.”
segments.s413-g76: گذشته سخنها is the object phrase “the past / former speeches/tales.” یاد کردن is expressed periphrastically: کرد is the past auxiliary/verb (“did/made”), and یاد is the noun “remembrance,” so کرد یاد = “he recalled.” The particle برو here functions as an archaic adverbial element (roughly “then”/“thereupon”) placed before کرد; word order is topical object → adverb → verb.
segments.s413-g77: برادرش combines the noun برادر (“brother”) with the possessive suffix ش (‘his’). را is the definite-object marker attached to the phrase; نیز means “also.” با خود is a prepositional phrase “with himself / beside him.” نشاند is an archaic past form meaning “set (someone) down / made sit” (here he caused his brother to sit beside him).
segments.s413-g78: وزان is a connective adverb meaning “and from that / thence.” نامداران کسان را is the object phrase “those renowned people” with را marking the definite object. نخواند is a past-tense form of خواندن used here in the sense “he called / summoned” (classical style places the verb after the object).
segments.s413-g79: دگر is an adjective meaning “another / other.” گور is a noun here meaning “grave / tomb.” بنهاد is a past verb (set up / placed / founded) used transitively, so the sequence means “he set another grave.” پیش تنش is a prepositional phrase meaning “before his body,” where تن (body) carries the possessive suffix ش (“his”).
segments.s413-g80: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که (“that / for / because”). هر بار is a temporal expression “each time / whenever.” گوری is the noun “grave” with the -ی suffix (here functioning as a generic/relational form), and بدی is a noun/adjective “evil / harm.” خوردنیش is خوردن (to take/receive/consume) with the pronominal/possessive/clitic suffix یش referring back to the person, so the verb-noun construction says the grave ‘took/received him’ in a harmful way — i.e. the grave brought suffering on him each time.
segments.s413-g1: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' placed before the noun. کوه (noun) + بد (adjective) is the noun followed by its describing adjective ('a bad/terrible mountain'). پیش مرد جوان is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before the young man' with پیش governing the noun phrase مرد جوان.
segments.s413-g2: برانگیخت is a past simple verb 'roused/raised'. آن باره را is the definite object (آن 'that' + باره 'occasion/affair') marked by را. پهلوان follows the verb phrase and functions as the agent/subject in this poetic word order (subject postposed after verb and object).
segments.s413-g3: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه 'gaze/watch' + کرد (past of کردن) = 'looked/turned his gaze'. به marks direction 'toward' and نخچیرگاه is the goal 'hunting-ground'. بهمن, the proper name, is the subject placed after the verb (poetic ordering).
segments.s413-g4: بدید is the past of دیدن 'saw'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' qualifying the following noun; بر here has the meaning 'upon / over' and combines with پهلوان سپاه to describe 'an army/host upon the champion' (word order in verse places the preposition before its object sequence).
segments.s413-g5: درختی is an indefinite/nominal form 'a tree'. گرفته is a past participle 'taken/holding' modifying the tree (the construction means 'a tree that has taken/held'). به + چنگ is an adverbial/instrument phrase 'with/by a claw' (به here rendered 'by/with'), and اندرون is the locative 'inside/within'.
segments.s413-g6: بر او نشسته is a participial phrase 'sitting on it/him' with او referring back to the tree. بسی is an intensifier 'much/many' in poetry (here 'many'), and رهنمون is a noun 'guides' or 'guidance' — together meaning 'many guides sat upon it.'
segments.s413-g7: یکی again = 'a certain'. نره is an adjective/noun 'male' and گوری here appears as an attributive (likely 'Ghorian' or a tribal/ethnic label in this context) forming 'a certain male Ghorian'. زده is the past participle/verb 'struck' and بر درخت gives the place/target 'on the tree'.
segments.s413-g8: نهاده is the past participle 'placed/put'. بر خویش is the reflexive phrase 'on himself' (خویش realized as خوی + ش), گوپال can be read as a proper name 'Gopāl' (or 'cowherd') and is coordinated with رخت 'baggage/clothing' by و 'and'. Grammatically this says he 'had placed upon himself Gopāl and (his) baggage.'
segments.s413-g49: پذیره شدش is a passive/periphrastic expression: پذیرِه (reception/acceptance) + شد + enclitic ش (to him) = 'he was received' or 'reception befell him'. با is the preposition 'with/by', زواره is a proper name here, and بهم is an adverb meaning 'together' — the small phrase literally reads 'he was received by Zavāreh together'. Word order is typical Persian/poetic: predicate before the agent phrase.
segments.s413-g50: به نخچیرگه marks direction/place ('into/ to the hunting-ground'); هرک (= هر که) is an indefinite 'each/whoever'. The phrase has no overt verb here and depends on the previous clause for the action (ellipsis common in poetry). بد modifies the indefinite ('bad' / 'ill'), and بیش و کم is an idiom 'more or less' (lit. 'more and less') qualifying the distribution or degree. Overall: 'to the hunting-ground each one, bad, more or less.'
segments.s413-g51: پیاده شد is a simple past 'he became on-foot' = 'he dismounted/ alighted'. از باره reads 'from the baggage/pack' (باره = load) and بهمن is a personal name attached in genitive-like sequence ('Bahman's'). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', followed by دود 'smoke' (the cause that triggers the next action). The clause order is compact/poetic: action + source + temporal clause.
segments.s413-g52: بپرسیدش is a past tense verb 'they asked him' (پرسیدن + enclitic ش = 'ask him'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. نیکویها is a plural substantive 'the good/excellent ones' used as a group noun, and فزود is a past intransitive verb 'increased/grew' (here, 'surged forward' or 'multiplied' in number or intensity). The sequence means 'they questioned him, and the noble/brave men increased/pressed forward.'
segments.s413-g53: بدو گفت رستم is an instance of inverted poetic word order: بدو 'to him' + گفت 'said' + رستم 'Rostam' (subject after verb). که introduces the subordinate clause. تا here means 'until', and نام خویش is 'your (own) name' — خوی + enclitic ش forms the possessive 'his/your own' in classical usage. The clause begins the conditional/time clause: 'that until (you) [say] your name…'.
segments.s413-g54: نگویی is a present-subjunctive/negative verb form from گفتن ('that you do not say' / 'unless you say' — here the verb expressing the act of naming is put in the subordinate clause). نیابی is a negative/result clause 'you will not find/attain' (یابیدن: 'attain/obtain') and is directed back to the speaker: ز من = 'from me'. کام خویش = 'your desire' (کام = desire; خوی + ش again is the possessive). The two verbs form a prototypical conditional: 'If you do not tell your name (lit. until you tell), you will not obtain your desire from me.'
segments.s413-g55: بدو گفت here again 'to him said' (he replied). من is the explicit first-person subject 'I'. پور + اسفندیار is a patronymic construction 'son of Esfandiyār' (پور used as 'son of' or 'descendant of'); the copula 'am' is omitted as is normal in Persian speech/poetry.
segments.s413-g56: سر here means 'chief/head' (a title), راستان is the adjective 'upright/righteous' (postpositive idiom is common), بهمن is a proper name, and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/illustrious'. Together they form a string of titles/epithets attached to the speaker's name: 'the upright chief, Bahmān, renowned.' Word order places epithets after the noun in the condensed, poetic style.

sentences.s413-s21: He sat aside from the swift-footed one; the pensive man sprang from the mountain and returned to his place.
sentences.s413-s22: He told that marvel to the mobed that he had seen, and from there withdrew into the easy path.
sentences.s413-s23: When he came near the hunting-ground, at that moment Tahmētan saw him from the road.
sentences.s413-s24: He said to the mobed thus: “Which man is this?” — I, having seen him, suppose that he is Goshtāsp.
sentences.s413-s33: At the auspicious, lofty king's command, he drew aside the inner curtain on the bank of the Helmand.
sentences.s413-s34: I will deliver a message from Esfandiyar, if the mounted champion hears.
sentences.s413-s35: Rostam said thus: 'By the king's command I am resolved to be above the sun and the moon.'
sentences.s413-s36: We will first eat what we have; afterwards the world lies under your command.
sentences.s413-s49: Someone filled a golden cup with wine and thereby brought to mind the free men.
sentences.s413-s50: He placed another cup into Bahman's hand, saying, 'Take from that man whom you wish to remember.'
sentences.s413-s51: Bahman was alarmed at the cup of wine; at the first instant he drew (it) in.
sentences.s413-s52: He said to him, 'O child of the king, may you be happy — wine and a cupbearer!'
sentences.s413-s17: Grief came upon Bahman's heart because of his deed when he saw that man's greatness and conduct.
sentences.s413-s18: One might say that the fortunate Esfandiyar would engage in so famed a battle.
sentences.s413-s19: He would bring disgrace upon his body in battle; it is better that he deal gently with him.
sentences.s413-s20: But if, perchance, fighting seems better to him, he will seize every city of Iran.
sentences.s413-s41: They sprinkled salt on it, cut it up, and ate it; the triumphant man watched straight ahead.
sentences.s413-s42: Bahman kept eating a little from the grave; there was not one of his hundred who ate of it.
sentences.s413-s43: Rostam laughed and said to him, 'O king, this court has that stew for him.'
sentences.s413-s44: If you have such a stew at your table, why did you go into the seven‑chambers (the seven trials)?
sentences.s413-s9: I fear that the champion Esfandiyar will not be able to stand against him and that his head will be twisted off in the battle.
sentences.s413-s10: I will make him lifeless with a single stone; I will make the hearts of Zal and Rudabeh twist (i.e., I will cause them grief).
sentences.s413-s11: He tore a single stone from that flinty mountain and hurled it down from that towering mountainside.
sentences.s413-s12: From his hunting-ground he saw Zovareh, and he heard the flinty stone roar.
sentences.s413-s13: A cry rang out, “O famed chief!” — a single rolling stone broke loose from the mountainside.
sentences.s413-s14: Rostam did not stir, nor did he set up a grave; Zovāreh kept making such a commotion.
sentences.s413-s15: It continued until the stone drew near; because of the whirl the mountain became dark.
sentences.s413-s16: He struck with his heel and cast the stone far; Zovāreh cried 'Bravo!' and (added) 'son.'
sentences.s413-s45: How did you thrust your spear in battle, when you have such eating habits, O king?
sentences.s413-s46: Bahman said to him: that Khosrow of noble birth (should) not be a mere talker and great eater.
sentences.s413-s47: There was little food; much toil and fighting (remained); then we will put our lives into our hands.
sentences.s413-s48: Rostam laughed aloud and said: 'It is not fitting that a man hide himself from other men.'
sentences.s413-s29: The champion quickly took him in his arms; because of the late arrival he accepted (his) apology.
sentences.s413-s30: Both departed and took their places: he himself and the renowned Khosrow‑worshippers.
sentences.s413-s31: When Bahman sat down, he gave him a greeting and added the salutations of the king and of the Iranians.
sentences.s413-s32: After that Kasfandiyar said thus: 'When the fire went forth from the king's gate...'
sentences.s413-s53: He seized that cup of Bahman from him in his grasp; having made hearts ache, he lingered on it.
sentences.s413-s54: He remained amazed at Rostam, astonished by that blow and by the mane, the arm, and the palm.
sentences.s413-s55: Both riders took their places (mounted); Bahman rode at the famed one.
sentences.s413-s56: He gave him, one by one, greetings and messages from Esfandiyar, that well‑renowned hero.
sentences.s413-s5: One held a cup brimming with wine in his hand; another — an admirer — had a youth prostrate at his feet before him.
sentences.s413-s6: His steed kept roaming in that meadow; there were trees and plants, and also a stream.
sentences.s413-s7: Bahman said in his heart, 'This is Rostam — or perhaps it is the sun at daybreak.'
sentences.s413-s8: No one in the world had ever seen a man like this, nor heard of any of the famed having precedence (over him).
sentences.s413-s37: He spread soft bread on the table-cloth; someone brought a hot roasted onager.
sentences.s413-s38: When the chanter stood before Bahman, he recalled the old tales.
sentences.s413-s39: He also seated his brother beside him, and summoned those renowned men.
sentences.s413-s40: He set another tomb before his body, for at every turn the grave would take him ill (i.e. the grave brought him suffering).
sentences.s413-s1: A certain bad/terrible mountain stood before the young man; the champion stirred up that affair.
sentences.s413-s2: Bahman turned his gaze to the hunting-ground and saw the champion's host.
sentences.s413-s3: A tree, having seized (something) with a claw inside, had many guides sitting upon it.
sentences.s413-s4: A certain male (Ghorian) struck the tree; he had placed upon himself Gopāl (or a cowherd named Gopāl) and his baggage.
sentences.s413-s25: He was received by Zavāreh together; to the hunting-ground he sent each one, more or less.
sentences.s413-s26: He dismounted from Bahman's baggage; when they saw smoke they questioned him, and the noble men surged forward.
sentences.s413-s27: Rostam said to him that until you tell your name you will not obtain your desire from me.
sentences.s413-s28: He replied to him, 'I am the son of Esfandiyār — Bahmān, renowned and upright.'
